title: Changi Terminal 5 Takes Shape as Singapore Builds for the 2030s
dek: With groundbreaking complete and inter-terminal tunnels finished, Singapore's Changi Airport pushed its Terminal 5 megaproject forward in early 2026, courting passengers with a public preview of a hub built to add 50 million annual travellers.
body:
Singapore's Changi Airport entered 2026 with its Terminal 5 megaproject visibly gathering pace, as the Changi East programme moved from planning into heavy civil works. Following a groundbreaking ceremony in 2025 and the award of major tunnelling, airfield and substructure contracts, the new terminal is being built to lift Changi's long-term capacity and cement Singapore's position as one of Asia's busiest aviation hubs. Authorities have signalled the terminal is targeting completion in the mid-2030s.

What is being built at Changi East

Terminal 5 is the centrepiece of the wider Changi East development, a multi-decade effort to expand the airport on land east of the existing terminals. When operational, T5 is expected to handle roughly 50 million passengers a year in its initial phase, a figure comparable to a large standalone airport in its own right.

Crucially, the new terminal is designed to function as part of one integrated airport rather than a detached satellite. Changi Airport Group has confirmed the completion of underground bored tunnels linking the existing terminal area with the future T5 site, infrastructure intended to carry baggage and automated people-mover trains so passengers and bags can move seamlessly across the campus.

A hub designed for resilience and connectivity

Planners have framed T5 around lessons from recent years, emphasising flexibility, pandemic resilience and the ability to scale capacity as demand recovers and grows. For travellers, the long-term promise is shorter transfers, more gates for widebody aircraft and additional headroom for the full-service and low-cost carriers that route through Singapore between Europe, the Gulf, South Asia, Southeast Asia and Australasia.

To keep the public engaged during the long build, Changi opened a preview experience titled "T5 In the Making" in early 2026, giving travellers a look at the terminal's scale and design ambitions well before any aircraft use it.

  • Targeted initial capacity of around 50 million passengers a year.
  • Underground tunnels for baggage and people-mover links already complete.
  • Designed as an integrated extension of Changi, not a separate facility.
  • Completion targeted for the mid-2030s, subject to construction progress.

For now, Terminal 5 remains a construction site rather than a boarding gate. But with foundational works advancing and connectivity tunnels in place, Singapore is making one of the clearest long-range capacity bets in Asian aviation, positioning Changi to compete with expanding Gulf and regional megahubs deep into the next decade.
